headline: Why France's national weather service struggles to give accurate heatwave predictions
subhead: Days ahead of the heatwaves of June and July, Météo-France slightly underestimated their intensity and duration. The reason has to do with limits in forecasting models for extreme events, as well as the challenge of explaining the uncertainty of weather predictions to the general public.

subhead: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio reopened a diplomatic campaign on Monday aimed at dismantling the ICC by combining sanctions, putting pressure on member states and seeking allies among major powers hostile to the institution.

headline: China's foreign trade surges, driven by AI and electric vehicles
subhead: China's exports rose by 27% in June compared to the same month last year. Despite an increase in imports, the country posted a trade surplus of $576 billion in the first half of the year.

headline: France celebrates Bastille Day, Macron's last as president, with parade showcasing unity with allies and Ukraine
subhead: The final July 14 parade presided over by President Emmanuel Macron, the event brought together thousands of French troops but also two dozen foreign heads of state and government, including Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky.

headline: 'Les Bleus represent France because France entrusted them with its place and they accepted it'
subhead: As players of the French national football team are targeted by racist remarks, political scientist and writer Rachid Benzine argues that confusion has arisen over what representation means, contending that it is not the same as resemblance.

headline: Is air conditioning good or bad? Join this (fake) group chat for (real) analysis
subhead: Each time a new heatwave hits, the question of air conditioning and its environmental impacts crops up in the public debate. Here are the key points to help you understand what is at stake.

headline: 'And I thought coke was for stressed-out executives!': Cocaine's ravages among manual workers
subhead: 'Cocaine at work' (3/3). Long associated with elite, urban circles, cocaine has now made its way deep into rural France and its mid-sized towns. On construction sites, farms and in warehouses, workers, some barely out of their teens, are turning to the drug to get through the physical toll of their jobs.
